description: An adult trauma patient arrives in the emergency department after a high-energy fall with a suspected femoral shaft fracture. They are conscious, distressed, and in severe pain with visible thigh deformity and early signs of blood loss. This scenario trains initial ED trauma assessment using ABCDE, monitoring, analgesia escalation, neurovascular limb assessment, safe limb handling, shock recognition, senior/orthopaedic escalation, imaging preparation, and structured handover.

clinicalContext.scopeOfPracticeNote: Trains assessment, prioritisation, escalation, monitoring, communication, and preparation for definitive care. It does not require exact drug dosing, fracture reduction, or surgery.
clinicalContext.importantNote: Internal physiology supports training decisions and debriefing only. Medication and intervention actions are abstracted.

guidelineAnchors.recognition: Severe thigh pain with deformity, swelling, shortening or rotation after high-energy trauma should be treated as a suspected femoral shaft fracture with risk of significant blood loss.

ABCDE.airway: Confirm the patient can speak and airway is patent. Consider C-spine precautions for significant trauma mechanisms.
ABCDE.breathing: Assess RR, work of breathing, chest injury signs, and SpO2.
ABCDE.circulation: Assess pulse, BP, capillary refill, skin signs, bleeding, shock risk. Prepare IV access, bloods, and group-and-save where appropriate.
ABCDE.disability: Assess AVPU/GCS, pain score, neurological symptoms, and response to analgesia.
ABCDE.exposure: Expose enough to inspect injury while maintaining dignity and preventing hypothermia.

clinicalManagement.earlyEscalation: Call senior ED/trauma help early for suspected femur fracture, severe pain, tachycardia, abnormal neurovascular findings, or shock risk.
clinicalManagement.avoidMovement: Do not straighten, lift, pull, or ask the patient to weight-bear.
clinicalManagement.neurovascularAssessment: Check distal pulse, capillary refill, sensation, and toe movement before and after immobilisation.
clinicalManagement.analgesia: Assess pain, request/administer appropriate analgesia within role/local policy, and reassess.
clinicalManagement.imagingAndReferral: Prepare imaging and orthopaedic referral after stabilisation.

patient.name: Alex Morgan
patient.mechanismOfInjury: brought to ED by ambulance after falling from an e-scooter at speed and landing heavily on the right side
patient.position: supine on ED trauma trolley, holding the right thigh and guarding against movement
patient.chiefComplaint: severe right thigh pain and unable to stand
visiblePresentation: awake and distressed on trauma trolley
right thigh visibly swollen and bruised
right leg appears shortened and externally rotated
skin becomes pale/cool/clammy if shock develops
patient.openingDialogue: My leg feels wrong. Please don't move it. It hurts so much.

pulseRate.label: Heart rate
pulseRate.meaning: Tachycardia can reflect pain, anxiety, blood loss, or shock.

bloodPressureSystolic.label: BP systolic
bloodPressureSystolic.meaning: Falling systolic blood pressure suggests developing shock.

respiratoryRate.label: Resp rate
respiratoryRate.meaning: May rise with pain, anxiety, or shock.

oxygenSaturation.label: SpO₂
oxygenSaturation.meaning: Usually initially normal in isolated femur fracture, but may fall with deterioration or poor perfusion.

consciousness.label: Consciousness
consciousness.meaning: 1 = alert, 0 = unresponsive. Falling consciousness is a danger sign.

painScore.label: Pain score
painScore.meaning: Severe pain should improve slightly with reassurance, stillness, support, and immobilisation.

temperature.label: Temperature
temperature.meaning: Trauma patients can cool quickly when lying still on the ground.

distalPerfusionDisplay.label: Distal CSM
distalPerfusionDisplay.meaning: Learner-visible summary after checking foot pulse, capillary refill, sensation, and toe movement.

couplings.reason: A painful unsupported femur fracture can worsen bleeding and shock risk.
Delaying emergency help increases the risk of deterioration before definitive care.
Blood loss and shock cause tachycardia, falling blood pressure, faster breathing, cooling, and reduced alertness.
Supporting the limb in the position found reduces pain and limits further movement.
Padding and immobilisation reduce movement, pain, and ongoing deterioration.
Keeping the patient warm supports trauma shock care.
Repeated unsafe handling of a suspected femur fracture increases pain, bleeding risk, and shock progression.
Worsening distal perfusion is a concerning limb finding and should be reassessed/escalated.

confirmIdentityAndIntroduceSelf.label: Confirm Identity / Introduce Self
confirmIdentityAndIntroduceSelf.instruction: Introduce yourself, confirm identity if possible, and explain that you will avoid unnecessary leg movement.
confirmIdentityAndIntroduceSelf.clinicalFinding: Patient is alert, frightened, and able to answer basic questions.
confirmIdentityAndIntroduceSelf.patientDialogue: Please don't move my leg. It feels wrong.
confirmIdentityAndIntroduceSelf.learnerFeedback: Good communication helps the patient cooperate and prevents sudden movement.

startABCDE.label: Start ABCDE Assessment
startABCDE.instruction: Begin a structured ABCDE assessment before focusing only on the leg.
startABCDE.clinicalFinding: Patient is awake, talking, breathing rapidly, pale, and in severe right thigh pain.
startABCDE.learnerFeedback: ABCDE prevents fixation on the obvious fracture.

assessAirwayCSpine.label: A — Assess Airway / Consider C-Spine
assessAirwayCSpine.instruction: Check speech/airway and consider C-spine precautions for the mechanism.
assessAirwayCSpine.clinicalFinding: Patient is speaking clearly. Airway is patent. High-energy fall means C-spine consideration is appropriate.
assessAirwayCSpine.patientDialogue: I can talk... it just really hurts.

assessBreathing.label: B — Assess Breathing
assessBreathing.instruction: Assess RR, work of breathing, chest signs, and SpO2.
assessBreathing.clinicalFinding: RR 22, SpO2 98% on air, equal chest movement, no obvious chest injury.

attachMonitoring.label: Attach ED Monitoring
attachMonitoring.instruction: Attach pulse oximeter, BP cuff, ECG leads, and start repeated observations.
attachMonitoring.clinicalFinding: Initial observations: HR 105, BP 112 systolic, RR 22, SpO2 98%, alert, pain 8/10.

assessCirculationBleeding.label: C — Assess Circulation / Bleeding
assessCirculationBleeding.instruction: Assess pulse, BP, capillary refill, skin signs, major external bleeding, and shock risk.
assessCirculationBleeding.clinicalFinding: Tachycardic, pale and anxious. No catastrophic external bleeding. Thigh swelling suggests possible internal blood loss.
assessCirculationBleeding.learnerFeedback: A closed femur fracture can still bleed internally.

establishIVAccess.label: Establish IV Access
establishIVAccess.instruction: Establish IV access within role/local policy, or request help to do so.
establishIVAccess.learnerFeedback: IV access supports analgesia, bloods, and shock management.

sendBloodsGroupAndSave.label: Send Bloods / Group and Save
sendBloodsGroupAndSave.instruction: Send trauma bloods and request group-and-save/crossmatch according to policy.
sendBloodsGroupAndSave.learnerFeedback: This prepares the team for deterioration or operative care.

assessDisabilityPain.label: D — Assess Disability / Pain
assessDisabilityPain.instruction: Assess AVPU/GCS, pain score, nausea, and neurological symptoms.
assessDisabilityPain.clinicalFinding: Patient is alert, orientated, pain 8/10, and reports tingling in the right foot.
assessDisabilityPain.patientDialogue: It's about an eight or nine out of ten. My foot feels a bit tingly.

requestAnalgesia.label: Request Appropriate Analgesia
requestAnalgesia.instruction: Request appropriate strong analgesia according to role and local policy.
requestAnalgesia.clinicalFinding: Analgesia requested due to severe pain and suspected femur fracture.
requestAnalgesia.learnerFeedback: Severe femur fracture pain needs active management.

administerAnalgesiaAsPrescribed.label: Administer Analgesia as Prescribed
administerAnalgesiaAsPrescribed.instruction: Administer prescribed/approved analgesia and monitor response, sedation, RR, BP, and nausea.
administerAnalgesiaAsPrescribed.clinicalFinding: Pain improves from 8/10 to around 6/10. Patient remains painful but less panicked.
administerAnalgesiaAsPrescribed.patientDialogue: That's helped a little. It still really hurts though.
administerAnalgesiaAsPrescribed.learnerFeedback: Analgesia reduces distress but does not fix the fracture.

exposeAndInspectInjury.label: E — Expose and Inspect Injury
exposeAndInspectInjury.instruction: Expose enough to inspect while maintaining dignity and preventing hypothermia. Do not lift or straighten the limb.
exposeAndInspectInjury.clinicalFinding: Right thigh is swollen, bruised, shortened, and externally rotated. No open wound seen.
exposeAndInspectInjury.patientDialogue: Please be careful. Don't lift it.

checkDistalPulse.label: Check Distal Pulse
checkDistalPulse.instruction: Assess distal circulation/sensation/movement without moving the injured leg.
checkDistalPulse.clinicalFinding: Pedal pulse is weak but present. Foot feels slightly cool.

checkCapillaryRefill.label: Check Capillary Refill
checkCapillaryRefill.instruction: Assess distal circulation/sensation/movement without moving the injured leg.
checkCapillaryRefill.clinicalFinding: Capillary refill in the injured foot is approximately 4 seconds.

checkSensation.label: Check Foot Sensation
checkSensation.instruction: Assess distal circulation/sensation/movement without moving the injured leg.
checkSensation.clinicalFinding: Patient can feel touch but reports pins and needles.
checkSensation.patientDialogue: I can feel that, but it's tingly.

checkToeMovement.label: Check Toe Movement
checkToeMovement.instruction: Assess distal circulation/sensation/movement without moving the injured leg.
checkToeMovement.clinicalFinding: Patient can wiggle toes slightly. Do not ask them to move the leg.
checkToeMovement.patientDialogue: I can wiggle my toes a little. Please don't ask me to lift the leg.

callSeniorTraumaHelp.label: Call Senior / Trauma Team
callSeniorTraumaHelp.instruction: Escalate: suspected femur fracture, severe pain, tachycardia, possible internal blood loss, weak distal pulse.
callSeniorTraumaHelp.learnerFeedback: This is a major trauma risk, not a simple limb injury.

supportLimbInPositionFound.label: Support Limb in Position Found
supportLimbInPositionFound.instruction: Support the injured leg exactly as found using pillows/foam/rolled blankets. Do not straighten it.
supportLimbInPositionFound.clinicalFinding: Leg is supported in the position found. Patient's guarding decreases slightly.
supportLimbInPositionFound.patientDialogue: That's a bit better. Please keep it still.
supportLimbInPositionFound.learnerFeedback: Supporting the limb reduces pain and prevents further injury.

immobiliseAndPad.label: Immobilise / Pad Around Limb
immobiliseAndPad.instruction: Immobilise/pad around the injured leg while keeping the foot accessible for repeat neurovascular checks.
immobiliseAndPad.clinicalFinding: Limb is padded and movement is reduced. Foot remains accessible for repeat CSM checks.
immobiliseAndPad.patientDialogue: It still hurts, but it feels less like it's moving.

repeatDistalCSMAfterImmobilisation.label: Repeat Distal CSM After Immobilisation
repeatDistalCSMAfterImmobilisation.instruction: Repeat distal pulse, cap refill, sensation, and toe movement after immobilisation.
repeatDistalCSMAfterImmobilisation.clinicalFinding: Pedal pulse remains weak but present. Capillary refill remains 4 seconds. Sensation still tingly but present.

prepareShockEscalation.label: Prepare Shock Escalation
prepareShockEscalation.instruction: Escalate possible haemorrhagic shock and prepare fluids/blood products according to local policy.
prepareShockEscalation.clinicalFinding: Shock escalation prepared. Senior team aware of blood loss concern.

requestXrayAndOrthoReferral.label: Request Imaging / Orthopaedics
requestXrayAndOrthoReferral.instruction: After stabilisation, request appropriate X-ray/imaging and contact orthopaedics.
requestXrayAndOrthoReferral.clinicalFinding: Femur/pelvis imaging requested and orthopaedics contacted.

reassessPainAndVitals.label: Reassess Pain and Vitals
reassessPainAndVitals.instruction: Reassess pain, sedation, RR, SpO2, HR, BP, consciousness, skin signs, and distal CSM trends.
reassessPainAndVitals.clinicalFinding: Pain improved to 6/10 after analgesia, HR 100, BP 112 systolic, RR 21, SpO2 98%, alert. Distal pulse remains weak but present.

prepareClinicalHandover.label: Prepare SBAR / ATMIST Handover
prepareClinicalHandover.instruction: Prepare SBAR/ATMIST including mechanism, injury, observations, neurovascular status, pain management, immobilisation, escalation, and concerns.
prepareClinicalHandover.clinicalFinding: Handover ready: suspected right femoral shaft fracture, severe pain, deformity/swelling, no open wound, weak distal pulse, cap refill 4s, tingling but movement present, analgesia given, limb supported/immobilised, monitoring attached, senior/ortho informed, imaging requested.

moveLeg.label: Move Injured Leg
moveLeg.instruction: Unsafe test action: moving the injured leg can worsen pain, bleeding, and tissue damage.
moveLeg.patientDialogue: OW! Stop! Stop! That really hurts!
moveLeg.learnerFeedback: Do not move a suspected femur fracture unless needed for immediate life-saving safety.

straightenLeg.label: Straighten Injured Leg
straightenLeg.instruction: Unsafe test action: do not straighten a deformed suspected femur fracture.
straightenLeg.patientDialogue: No! Stop! You're making it worse!
straightenLeg.learnerFeedback: Straightening the limb can worsen pain, bleeding, and neurovascular compromise.

askPatientToStand.label: Ask Patient to Stand
askPatientToStand.instruction: Unsafe test action: do not ask the patient to weight-bear on a suspected femur fracture.
askPatientToStand.patientDialogue: I can't stand — my leg feels wrong!
askPatientToStand.learnerFeedback: Do not test weight-bearing in a clearly deformed, painful femur injury.

applyTightThighBandage.label: Apply Tight Bandage Over Thigh
applyTightThighBandage.instruction: Unsafe test action: do not apply tight circumferential pressure over a closed deformed thigh without a clear external bleed.
applyTightThighBandage.patientDialogue: That's too tight — my foot feels colder!
applyTightThighBandage.learnerFeedback: Tight bandaging can worsen distal circulation. Keep the foot accessible for repeat CSM checks.

giveFoodOrDrink.label: Give Food or Drink
giveFoodOrDrink.instruction: Unsafe test action: avoid food or drink because the patient may need surgery, sedation, or urgent procedures.
giveFoodOrDrink.patientDialogue: I'm thirsty, but I feel a bit sick.
giveFoodOrDrink.learnerFeedback: Avoid giving food or drink while awaiting emergency care.

gold.description: ABCDE, monitoring, senior escalation, analgesia, distal CSM before/after immobilisation, limb stabilisation, shock escalation, imaging/ortho referral, and handover completed safely.
gold.outcome: Patient stabilised in ED trauma bay awaiting definitive orthopaedic management.

unstable_completion.description: The required clinical pathway was completed, but the patient remains unstable or the care included unsafe actions/poor-quality performance.
unstable_completion.outcome: Patient remains clinically unstable. Proceed to debrief focusing on unsafe handling, care quality, pain control, shock risk, and escalation.

delayed_senior_escalation.debriefMessage: Delay in calling senior/trauma help allowed avoidable deterioration.

no_monitoring.debriefMessage: Monitoring was not attached early, so deterioration trends could be missed.

untreated_severe_pain.debriefMessage: Severe femur fracture pain was not actively managed or reassessed.

unsafe_movement.debriefMessage: Moving or straightening the injured leg worsened pain and shock risk.

no_distal_csm.debriefMessage: Distal CSM was not checked before/after immobilisation or handed over.

repeated_unsafe_limb_handling.debriefMessage: Repeated unsafe handling of the injured limb worsened pain, blood loss risk, and possible neurovascular compromise.

shockDeterioration.title: Patient Deteriorated
shockDeterioration.message: Patient deteriorated with signs of haemorrhagic shock or repeated unsafe limb handling.

unstableClinicalCompletion.title: Patient Remains Clinically Unstable
unstableClinicalCompletion.message: The clinical pathway was completed, but the patient remains unstable or care quality was unsafe/suboptimal. Review the debrief for unsafe limb handling, pain control, shock recognition, escalation, and reassessment points.

successfulClinicalStabilisation.title: ED Trauma Stabilisation Achieved
successfulClinicalStabilisation.message: Patient stabilised in the ED trauma bay. The fracture is not definitively treated, but pain, monitoring, neurovascular assessment, safe limb handling, shock risk, escalation, imaging preparation, and handover have been managed safely.

dialogueDesign.approach: Hybrid: keep short deterministic safety-critical patient lines in this scenario JSON, and store richer adaptive conversation in Pillar 4 LLM dialogue files.
keepInScenarioJson: pain/distress response to unsafe limb movement
simple findings linked to examination actions
handover-critical symptom statements such as tingling or cold foot
dialogueDesign.reason: The scenario engine needs predictable, testable lines for clinical findings and unsafe-action feedback. Pillar 4 can add natural conversation without breaking assessment logic.

outcomeDesign.rationale: Critical deterioration should end the case before partial/success. A completed but poor-quality pathway should end as unstable rather than continue running indefinitely. Clean success requires stable physiology, no unsafe limb handling, and adequate average action quality.
outcomeDesign.qualityHandling: Poor actions may still mark the action as completed, but action quality should feed averageActionQuality for outcome selection and debrief.
